items.subtopic: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT
items.title: Why is a thorough occupational history crucial in diagnosing lung disease?
items.content: A detailed occupational history can reveal the underlying workplace or environmental exposure contributing to respiratory conditions, which may present similarly to non-environmental diseases. Identifying these exposures is essential for patient management, prognosis, and legal implications.
keyPoints: Occupational and environmental factors significantly impact patient management and prognosis.
15–20% of adult asthma and COPD burden is estimated due to occupational factors.
A careful exposure history can uncover workplace or general environmental exposures.
Knowledge of etiology may have legal and financial implications for patients.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT

items.subtopic: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT
items.title: How to effectively assess occupational and environmental exposure in lung disease?
items.content: Assessing potential exposures involves detailed inquiries about work practices, contaminants, workspace conditions, use of protective equipment, and coworker symptoms. Consideration of alternative exposure sources and historical changes in workplace processes is also crucial.
keyPoints: Inquire about specific contaminants, visible dust, chemical odors, workspace size, ventilation, and respiratory protection.
Temporal association between work exposure and symptoms can indicate occupation-related disease.
Consider hobbies, home environment, secondhand smoke, and proximity to industrial facilities as alternative exposures.
Use OSHA regulations for workplace hazard information and consider site visits or inspections.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT

items.title: Detecting Occupational Asthma with FEV1 Measurements
items.content: Measurement of forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1) before and after a working shift can be used to detect an acute bronchoconstrictive response, indicative of occupational asthma.
keyPoints: Occupational exposures to dusts or chemical agents may cause airway obstruction characteristic of occupational asthma.
Use FEV1 measurements pre- and post-work shift to identify acute bronchoconstriction.
This method helps in diagnosing occupational asthma by detecting changes in lung function related to work exposure.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · LABORATORY TESTS

items.title: Role of CT Scans in Diagnosing Environmentally Induced Lung Disease
items.content: Conventional computed tomography (CT) and high-resolution CT (HRCT) are more sensitive than chest radiographs for detecting lung opacities, pleural thickening, and interstitial changes in patients with a history of dust exposure.
keyPoints: Chest radiographs can be useful but may underestimate severity in diseases like asbestosis.
Conventional CT detects lung opacities and pleural thickening more effectively than chest X-rays.
HRCT is particularly beneficial for identifying interstitial changes, improving diagnostic accuracy.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · LABORATORY TESTS

items.title: How Particle Size Affects Respiratory Deposition and Disease Risk
items.content: The size of inhaled particles significantly influences where they deposit within the respiratory tract, affecting disease risk. Larger particles (>10–15 μm) are trapped in the nose and throat, while those <10 μm can reach below the larynx. Fine-mode particles (<2.5 μm), primarily from combustion processes, dominate surface area exposure in the lower airways. Ultrafine particles (<0.1 μm) may enter circulation if deposited.
keyPoints: Particles >10–15 μm do not penetrate beyond the nose and throat.
Particles <10 μm deposit below the larynx.
Fine-mode fraction (2.5–10 μm) contains crustal elements like silica, aluminum, iron; deposits in tracheobronchial tree.
Fine particles (<2.5 μm) dominate surface area exposure and are linked to combustion processes.
Ultrafine particles (<0.1 μm) can penetrate into circulation if deposited.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · DETERMINANTS OF INHALATIONAL EXPOSURE

items.title: Impact of Gas Solubility on Respiratory Tract Irritation and Injury
items.content: The solubility of inhaled gases determines their absorption site within the respiratory tract, influencing irritation and injury. Water-soluble gases like ammonia and sulfur dioxide are absorbed in upper airways, causing irritative responses. Less soluble gases such as nitrogen dioxide and phosgene penetrate deeper to bronchioles and alveoli, potentially leading to acute chemical pneumonitis.
keyPoints: Water-soluble gases (e.g., ammonia, sulfur dioxide) are absorbed in upper airways.
Less soluble gases (e.g., nitrogen dioxide, phosgene) can reach bronchioles and alveoli.
Solubility affects the site of deposition and type of respiratory response.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · DETERMINANTS OF INHALATIONAL EXPOSURE

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES
items.title: Asbestos Exposure and Associated Diseases
items.content: Asbestos exposure can occur in various occupational settings, including mining, manufacturing, shipbuilding, construction, and even among bystanders. The major health effects include pleural and pulmonary fibrosis, respiratory tract cancers, and mesothelioma.
keyPoints: Exposure to asbestos is not limited to direct handlers; it includes bystanders like painters and electricians.
Major health effects: pleural/pulmonary fibrosis, respiratory tract cancers, pleural/peritoneal mesothelioma.
Asbestos has been largely replaced in the developed world but continues to be used in developing countries.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES
items.title: Diagnostic Features of Asbestosis
items.content: Asbestosis is characterized by pleural plaques, irregular or linear opacities in the lower lung fields, and a restrictive pattern on pulmonary function tests. HRCT may show subpleural curvilinear lines.
keyPoints: Pleural plaques indicate exposure but not necessarily impairment.
Chest radiographic hallmarks: irregular/linear opacities, indistinct heart border, ground-glass appearance.
HRCT findings: subpleural curvilinear lines 5–10 mm in length parallel to the pleural surface.
Pulmonary function tests show a restrictive pattern with decreased lung volumes and diffusing capacity.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES
items.title: Asbestos Exposure and Cancer Risk
items.content: Lung cancer is the most common asbestos-related cancer, with a latency period of 15–19 years. Smoking significantly increases risk. Mesotheliomas can develop from short-term exposures up to 40 years prior.
keyPoints: Lung cancer: minimum latency of 15–19 years post-exposure.
Smoking and asbestos exposure have an interactive effect, increasing lung cancer risk beyond additive effects.
Mesothelioma associated with asbestos exposure can develop from exposures as short as 1-2 years, up to 40 years ago.
>80% of mesotheliomas are linked to asbestos exposure.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES

items.title: Acute Silicosis: Rapid Onset and Severe Progression
items.content: Acute silicosis can develop rapidly, often within months of exposure in high-risk occupations such as sandblasting or working with artificial stone countertops. The condition is severe and progressive even after stopping exposure.
keyPoints: Rapid onset in confined spaces or high quartz content environments (15–25%)
Clinical features similar to pulmonary alveolar proteinosis
Chest radiograph may show miliary infiltration or 'crazy paving' pattern on HRCT
Progression despite discontinuation of exposure
Whole-lung lavage can provide symptomatic relief and slow progression
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · SILICOSIS

items.title: Complicated Silicosis: Progressive Massive Fibrosis and Associated Risks
items.content: Long-term, less intense exposure to silica leads to simple silicosis, characterized by small rounded opacities. Complicated silicosis involves larger nodules (>1 cm) forming progressive massive fibrosis (PMF), which can cause significant lung function impairment.
keyPoints: Simple silicosis: Small rounded opacities in upper lobes after 15–20 years, often without functional impairment
Calcification of hilar nodes in up to 20% of cases, producing an 'eggshell' pattern
Complicated silicosis: Irregular masses >1 cm forming PMF
PMF associated with both restrictive and obstructive lung function impairments
Increased risk of lung infections (e.g., tuberculosis) and autoimmune disorders due to alveolar macrophage dysfunction
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · SILICOSIS

items.title: Prevalence and Risk Factors for CWP
items.content: Coal worker's pneumoconiosis (CWP) is a significant occupational lung disease among coal miners, with varying prevalence based on the type of coal mined. Simple radiographically identified CWP affects approximately 10% of all coal miners and up to 50% of anthracite miners with more than 20 years of exposure. The presence of silica in anthracitic coal dust increases the risk for progressive massive fibrosis (PMF), especially as mechanization leads to increased silica contamination.
keyPoints: Simple CWP prevalence: ~10% of all coal miners, up to 50% of anthracite miners with >20 years of work
Lower prevalence in bituminous coal mines compared to anthracite
Risk factors for PMF include silica contamination and prolonged exposure (15–20 years)
Increased mechanization contributes to higher silica levels in mine dust
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · COAL WORKER'S PNEUMOCONIOSIS (CWP)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Complications of CWP
items.content: CWP can manifest as simple or complicated forms, with the latter characterized by nodules ≥1 cm in diameter on chest radiographs, typically in the upper lungs. This condition can progress to progressive massive fibrosis (PMF), leading to severe pulmonary impairment and premature mortality. Coal dust exposure also contributes to chronic bronchitis and COPD, with additive effects from cigarette smoking.
keyPoints: Complicated CWP: nodules ≥1 cm in diameter, usually in the upper lungs
Progression to PMF can cause severe lung function deficits and premature mortality
Coal dust exposure is also linked to chronic bronchitis and COPD
Additive effects of coal dust and cigarette smoking on respiratory health
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · COAL WORKER'S PNEUMOCONIOSIS (CWP)

items.title: Diagnosing Chronic Beryllium Disease: Importance of BeLPT and Biopsy
items.content: Chronic beryllium disease (CBD) is distinguished from sarcoidosis by a specific cell-mediated immune response to beryllium, confirmed through the beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test (BeLPT). Diagnosis often requires fiberoptic bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy to identify noncaseating granulomas or monocytic infiltration in lung tissue.
keyPoints: CBD is distinguished from sarcoidosis by a specific cell-mediated immune response to beryllium.
The BeLPT measures lymphocyte proliferation in the presence of beryllium salts, indicating sensitization.
Diagnosis often requires fiberoptic bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy showing noncaseating granulomas or monocytic infiltration.
Accumulation of beryllium-specific CD4+ T cells occurs in granulomatous inflammation on lung biopsy.
Susceptibility to CBD is associated with HLA-DP alleles having a glutamic acid in position 69 of the β chain.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · CHRONIC BERYLLIUM DISEASE

items.title: Etiology and Clinical Presentation of Chronic Beryllium Disease
items.content: Chronic beryllium disease (CBD) is a granulomatous inflammatory condition linked to occupational exposure to beryllium. It presents similarly to sarcoidosis but with less common hilar adenopathy. Early stages may show normal chest imaging and pulmonary function tests.
keyPoints: Beryllium exposure in alloys, ceramics, or electronics manufacturing can lead to CBD.
CBD is similar to sarcoidosis but with less common hilar adenopathy on imaging.
Chest imaging findings include nodules along septal lines; pulmonary function tests may show restrictive/obstructive deficits and decreased diffusing capacity.
Early disease stages may present with normal chest imaging and pulmonary function tests.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · CHRONIC BERYLLIUM DISEASE

items.title: Consider occupational exposures when diagnosing interstitial lung disease or sarcoidosis.
items.content: Exposure to metals such as tungsten carbide and cobalt, commonly found in tool manufacturing and diamond polishing, can lead to giant cell interstitial pneumonitis and occupational asthma. When evaluating patients with interstitial lung disease or a suspected diagnosis of sarcoidosis, it is crucial to inquire about exposure to metal fumes and dusts.
keyPoints: Tungsten carbide exposure may cause giant cell interstitial pneumonitis.
Cobalt, a component of tungsten carbide, can lead to occupational asthma and interstitial pneumonitis.
Common exposures include tool manufacturing, saw blade production, drill bit manufacture, and diamond polishing.
In patients with interstitial lung disease or sarcoidosis, inquire about metal fume/dust exposure.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ☐ OTHER METALS | OTHER INORGANIC DUSTS

items.title: Assess the additive effects of dust exposure and smoking in chronic bronchitis and COPD.
items.content: Inorganic dusts can lead to chronic mucus hypersecretion, similar to cigarette smoke, contributing to chronic bronchitis and COPD. When evaluating these conditions, consider both occupational/environmental exposures and smoking history due to their additive effects on lung pathology.
keyPoints: Inorganic dusts contribute to chronic mucus hypersecretion and may lead to chronic bronchitis and COPD.
Cigarette smoking is the major cause of these conditions.
Occupational and environmental exposures can have an additive effect with smoking.
Small airway inflammation is likely the initial pathologic response in dust exposure cases.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ☐ OTHER METALS | OTHER INORGANIC DUSTS

items.title: Byssinosis: Recognizing Occupational Asthma in Textile Workers
items.content: Byssinosis, an asthma-like syndrome, is caused by exposure to cotton dust and endotoxins. It manifests as chest tightness at the end of a workweek, particularly on Mondays ('Monday chest tightness'). Over time, symptoms may persist throughout the week, with obstructive patterns evident in pulmonary function tests after >10 years of exposure.
keyPoints: Chest tightness occurs toward the end of the first day of the workweek.
Significant drop in FEV1 over a Monday shift is common initially.
After >10 years, recurrent symptoms are more likely to show an obstructive pattern on pulmonary function testing.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ORGANIC DUSTS

items.title: Grain Dust Exposure: Identifying Occupational Respiratory Risks
items.content: Exposure to grain dust in farmers and workers can lead to obstructive airway disease, similar to symptoms seen in cigarette smokers. Symptoms include persistent cough, mucus hypersecretion, wheeze, dyspnea on exertion, and reduced FEV1 and FEV1/FVC ratio. The risk is additive with smoking, affecting ~50% of workers who smoke.
keyPoints: Symptoms: persistent cough, mucus hypersecretion, wheeze, dyspnea on exertion.
Reduced FEV1 and FEV1/FVC ratio observed.
~50% of grain dust-exposed workers who smoke exhibit symptoms.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ORGANIC DUSTS

items.title: Farmer's Lung: Diagnosing Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis from Moldy Hay
items.content: Farmer's lung is caused by exposure to moldy hay containing spores of thermophilic actinomycetes, leading to hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Acute cases present 4–8 hours post-exposure with fever, chills, malaise, cough, and dyspnea without wheezing. Chronic forms involve repeated attacks after similar exposures.
keyPoints: Acute presentation: 4-8 hours post-exposure with fever, chills, malaise, cough, dyspnea.
Chronic form involves repeated attacks after exposure.
Differentiation from influenza or pneumonia is crucial.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ORGANIC DUSTS

items.title: Smoke Inhalation in Fire Victims: A Leading Cause of Mortality
items.content: Smoke inhalation is a critical cause of acute cardiorespiratory failure and death among fire victims, often more lethal than thermal injuries. Carbon monoxide poisoning leading to significant hypoxemia can be life-threatening.
keyPoints: Smoke inhalation is the primary cause of mortality in fire victims.
Carbon monoxide exposure results in significant hypoxemia, which can be fatal.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · TOXIC CHEMICALS

items.title: Occupational Asthma from Reactive Agents
items.content: Exposure to reactive agents like diisocyanates and acid anhydrides in industrial settings is linked with occupational asthma, which may not involve IgE antibody-mediated mechanisms.
keyPoints: Reactive agents such as diisocyanates and acid anhydrides pose a high risk of occupational asthma.
Occupational asthma from these exposures can occur without IgE antibody involvement.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · TOXIC CHEMICALS

items.title: Health Risks from Burn Pit Emissions for Military Personnel
items.content: Military personnel exposed to burn pit emissions during deployments in the Middle East and Southwest Asia have reported respiratory symptoms. Legislation now provides disability compensation for presumptive conditions related to such exposures.
keyPoints: Burn pits used by the military burned various materials, releasing toxic agents.
Veterans exposed to burn pit emissions may develop conditions like asthma, COPD, and interstitial lung diseases.
U.S. legislation now compensates veterans for health issues presumed related to burn pit exposure.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · TOXIC CHEMICALS

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY
items.title: Occupational Exposures and Lung Cancer Risk
items.content: Workplace exposures contribute to a significant portion of lung cancer cases, with 10% attributed to occupational respiratory carcinogens. Various agents are implicated, including asbestos, acrylonitrile, arsenic compounds, beryllium, bis(chloromethyl) ether, chromium (hexavalent), formaldehyde, isopropanol, mustard gas, nickel carbonyl, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, secondhand tobacco smoke, silica, talc, vinyl chloride, wood dust, and uranium. Additionally, workers in certain mining operations face risks from radiation-related lung cancer due to radon exposure.
keyPoints: 10% of all lung cancer cases are attributed to occupational exposures.

Known respiratory carcinogens include asbestos, acrylonitrile, arsenic compounds, beryllium, bis(chloromethyl) ether, chromium (hexavalent), formaldehyde, isopropanol, mustard gas, nickel carbonyl, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, secondhand tobacco smoke, silica, talc, vinyl chloride, wood dust, and uranium.
Radiation-related lung cancer risk from radon exposure in mining operations.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY
items.title: Assessment of Disability Due to Respiratory Impairment
items.content: Disability assessment involves evaluating the decreased ability to work due to medical conditions, incorporating both physiological dysfunction and nonmedical factors like education and employability. Different agencies have varying criteria for disability compensation; for instance, the U.S. Social Security Administration requires total disability for income replacement, while some state workers' compensation systems allow partial disability payments. Pulmonary function tests (spirometry and diffusing capacity) are primary tools for assessing respiratory impairment, with cardiopulmonary exercise testing used if resting test results do not match symptoms. Methacholine challenge tests may be useful in asthma patients with normal spirometry.
keyPoints: Disability assessment includes physiological dysfunction and nonmedical factors such as education and employability.
U.S. Social Security requires total disability for income replacement, while some state systems allow partial disability payments.
Pulmonary function tests (spirometry and diffusing capacity) are primary tools for respiratory impairment assessment.
Cardiopulmonary exercise testing is used if resting test results do not correlate with symptoms.
Methacholine challenge tests can be useful in asthma patients with normal spirometry.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY

items.title: Impact of Air Pollution on Health: Mechanisms and Public Health Measures
items.content: Outdoor air pollution, regulated by the EPA for pollutants like sulfur dioxide, particulate matter (PM), nitrogen dioxide, ozone, lead, and carbon monoxide, has significant health impacts. Pollutants from stationary and mobile sources undergo chemical reactions in the atmosphere, leading to conditions such as smog. Chronic exposure to these pollutants is linked to decreased lung function growth, asthma, increased cardiopulmonary morbidity, and mortality.
keyPoints: Pollutants regulated by EPA include sulfur dioxide, PM, nitrogen dioxide, ozone, lead, and carbon monoxide.
Chemical reactions in the atmosphere can transform pollutants (e.g., sulfur dioxide to acid sulfate aerosols).
Fine particulate matter causes oxidative stress, local lung injury, inflammation, and systemic inflammatory responses.
Stricter U.S. air quality standards have been implemented for ozone, oxides of nitrogen, and PM.
Public health measures include pollution alerts and creating clean air spaces with central ventilation filtration or portable HEPA air cleaners.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OUTDOOR AIR POLLUTION

items.title: Epidemiologic Evidence Linking Air Pollution to Cardiopulmonary Health Risks
items.content: Air pollution is associated with health conditions similar to those caused by cigarette smoking, including asthma and decreased lung function growth. Population studies have shown increased healthcare utilization for cardiopulmonary issues and higher mortality rates in areas with elevated air pollutant levels. Cohort studies indicate excess morbidity and mortality from cardiopulmonary conditions in cities with high particulate exposure.
keyPoints: Air pollution symptoms are similar to those caused by cigarette smoking.
Chronic exposure to traffic-related air pollution is linked to asthma and decreased lung function growth.
Population-based studies show excess healthcare utilization for asthma and cardiopulmonary conditions during high pollution episodes.
Cities with higher particulate exposures have increased morbidity and mortality from cardiopulmonary diseases.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OUTDOOR AIR POLLUTION

items.title: Secondhand Tobacco Smoke and Respiratory Risk
items.content: Exposure to secondhand tobacco smoke significantly increases the risk of respiratory illnesses, particularly in children. The relative risk for lung cancer and cardiopulmonary diseases is increased by approximately 25% due to secondhand smoke exposure.
keyPoints: Respirable particulate load correlates with number of smokers at home
~25% increase in relative risk for lung cancer and cardiopulular diseases from secondhand smoke, even after adjusting for confounders
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · INDOOR EXPOSURES

items.title: Radon Gas as a Lung Cancer Risk Factor
items.content: Radon gas exposure in homes is linked to an increased risk of lung cancer. The problem is exacerbated when smokers are present, due to the molecular size of radon particles attaching readily to smoke particles.
keyPoints: Radon-222 from uranium-238 decay series
10% of U.S. houses may have excess lung cancer risk levels
Risk greater in homes with smokers
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · INDOOR EXPOSURES

items.title: Household Air Pollution and Global Health Impact
items.content: Exposure to household air pollution from cooking or heating with solid fuels is a significant global health hazard, contributing to deaths worldwide. It affects respiratory health in children and adults and increases the risk of chronic diseases.
keyPoints: Responsible for 4% of global deaths due to pneumonia, COPD, lung cancer, cardiovascular disease
40% of world's population uses solid fuel for domestic purposes
24-h mean levels of fine PM can be 2–30 times higher than U.S. EPA standards in homes using solid fuels
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · INDOOR EXPOSURES

items.subtopic: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT
items.question: Why is a careful occupational history important in the management of patients with lung disease?
options: It helps identify potential workplace exposures that may be causing or exacerbating the disease.
It allows for immediate cessation of all work-related activities without further investigation.
It ensures that patients are prescribed the correct medication based on their job title.
It is only necessary if the patient has a history of smoking.
items.explanation: A careful occupational history helps identify potential workplace exposures that may be causing or exacerbating the disease, which can significantly affect management and prognosis.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT

items.subtopic: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT
items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically included in an inquiry about specific work practices?
options: Questions about visible dust or chemical odors.
The size and ventilation of workspaces.
items.explanation: Inquiries into specific work practices typically do not include questions about the patient's dietary habits, as they are unrelated to occupational exposures.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT

items.subtopic: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT
items.question: What is one potential outcome of establishing an occupational etiology for a lung disease?
options: The patient may need to cease exposure to the offending agent.
The patient will automatically receive financial compensation.
All coworkers must be tested for the same disease.
The patient can continue working without any changes.
items.explanation: Establishing an occupational etiology often requires cessation of exposure to the offending agent, which is crucial for managing conditions like occupational asthma or hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT

items.question: Which imaging technique is more sensitive than a chest radiograph for detecting lung opacities and pleural thickening in patients with a history of dust exposure?
items.explanation: Conventional CT is more sensitive than chest radiographs, and HRCT further improves detection of interstitial changes.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · LABORATORY TESTS

items.question: What pattern on a chest radiograph is typically associated with asbestosis?
items.explanation: Asbestosis is characterized by small linear opacities on a chest radiograph.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · LABORATORY TESTS

items.question: Which test can be used to detect an acute bronchoconstrictive response in workers exposed to dusts or chemical agents?
options: Measurement of change in FEV1 before and after a working shift
Skin prick testing for immediate hypersensitivity
items.explanation: Change in forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1) before and after a working shift can detect an acute bronchoconstrictive response.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · LABORATORY TESTS

items.question: Which type of gases are absorbed in the lining fluid of the upper and proximal airways, causing irritative and bronchoconstrictive responses?
options: Water-soluble gases such as ammonia and sulfur dioxide
items.explanation: Water-soluble gases like ammonia and sulfur dioxide are absorbed in the upper airways, causing irritation.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · DETERMINANTS OF INHALATIONAL EXPOSURE

items.question: Which particles tend to deposit relatively high in the tracheobronchial tree?
items.explanation: Coarse particles (~2.5–10 μm) deposit high in the tracheobronchial tree.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · DETERMINANTS OF INHALATIONAL EXPOSURE

items.question: Which particle size is most likely to penetrate into the circulation and be carried to extrapulmonary sites?
items.explanation: Ultrafine particles (<0.1 μm) can penetrate into circulation and reach extrapulmonary sites.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · DETERMINANTS OF INHALATIONAL EXPOSURE

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a known health effect from asbestos exposure?
items.explanation: Asbestos exposure is known to cause pleural and pulmonary fibrosis, cancers of the respiratory tract, and benign pleural effusions, but not cardiac arrhythmias.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES
items.question: What is a common radiographic finding in asbestosis?
items.explanation: Pleural plaques are a common radiographic finding in asbestosis, indicating past asbestos exposure.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES
items.question: Which of the following is true about mesothelioma and smoking?
options: Smoking does not affect the risk of developing mesothelioma.
Smoking decreases the risk of mesothelioma.
items.explanation: Smoking does not affect the risk of developing mesothelioma, as these tumors are not associated with smoking.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES
items.question: What is a characteristic feature of pulmonary function testing in asbestosis?
options: Obstructive pattern with increased lung volumes
Restrictive pattern with decreased lung volumes
Normal lung volumes and diffusing capacity
Increased diffusing capacity with normal lung volumes
items.explanation: Pulmonary function testing in asbestosis reveals a restrictive pattern with a decrease in both lung volumes and diffusing capacity.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES

items.question: Which of the following occupations is NOT typically associated with exposure to free silica?
items.explanation: Textile weaving is not mentioned as an occupation associated with silica exposure.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · SILICOSIS

items.question: What radiographic pattern may be observed in acute silicosis?
items.explanation: 'Crazy paving' is a characteristic HRCT pattern observed in acute silicosis.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · SILICOSIS

items.question: Which of the following conditions can develop due to long-term, less intense exposure to silica?
items.explanation: 'Simple silicosis' is associated with long-term, less intense exposure to silica.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · SILICOSIS

items.question: Which condition in coal miners is associated with the presence of nodules ≥1 cm in diameter on chest radiographs?
items.explanation: Complicated CWP is characterized by nodules ≥1 cm in diameter on chest radiographs.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · COAL WORKER'S PNEUMOCONIOSIS (CWP)

items.question: What condition is described as the combination of pneumoconiotic nodules and seropositive rheumatoid arthritis?
items.explanation: Caplan syndrome is the combination of pneumoconiotic nodules and seropositive rheumatoid arthritis.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · COAL WORKER'S PNEUMOCONIOSIS (CWP)

items.question: Which type of coal miners have a higher prevalence of CWP, as mentioned in the passage?
options: Anthracite miners with >20 years on the coal face
items.explanation: Anthracite miners with more than 20 years of work have a higher prevalence of CWP.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · COAL WORKER'S PNEUMOCONIOSIS (CWP)

items.question: Which test is used to provide evidence of a specific cell-mediated immune response to beryllium in chronic beryllium disease?
items.explanation: The beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test (BeLPT) is used to detect a specific cell-mediated immune response in chronic beryllium disease.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · CHRONIC BERYLLIUM DISEASE

items.question: What type of granulomas are found in lung tissue of individuals with chronic beryllium disease?
items.explanation: Chronic beryllium disease is characterized by the presence of noncaseating granulomas in lung tissue.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · CHRONIC BERYLLIUM DISEASE

items.question: Which human leukocyte antigen (HLA) allele is highly associated with susceptibility to chronic beryllium disease?
items.explanation: Susceptibility to chronic beryllium disease is highly associated with HLA-DP alleles that have a glutamic acid in position 69 of the beta chain.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · CHRONIC BERYLLIUM DISEASE

items.question: Which metal is the likely etiologic agent of interstitial pneumonitis and occupational asthma associated with tungsten carbide exposure?
items.explanation: Cobalt is a constituent of tungsten carbide and is the likely etiologic agent.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ☐ OTHER METALS | OTHER INORGANIC DUSTS

items.question: What type of lung disease may be produced by exposure to dust containing tungsten carbide?
items.explanation: Exposure to dust containing tungsten carbide may produce giant cell interstitial pneumonitis.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ☐ OTHER METALS | OTHER INORGANIC DUSTS

items.question: Which of the following is a common exposure source for tungsten carbide?
items.explanation: The most common exposures to tungsten carbide occur in tool and dye, saw blade, and drill bit manufacture.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ☐ OTHER METALS | OTHER INORGANIC DUSTS

items.question: Which occupational exposure is associated with an asthma-like syndrome known as byssinosis?
items.explanation: Byssinosis is specifically linked to exposure to cotton dust in the production of yarns for textiles and rope making.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ORGANIC DUSTS

items.question: What symptom characterizes early-stage byssinosis?
options: Chest tightness at any time during the week
Occasional chest tightness toward the end of the first day of the workweek
items.explanation: Early-stage byssinosis is characterized by occasional chest tightness, typically occurring at the end of the first day of the workweek.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ORGANIC DUSTS

items.question: What pulmonary function test finding is more likely in workers with recurrent symptoms after >10 years of exposure to cotton dust?
options: Obstructive pattern on pulmonary function testing
items.explanation: Workers with recurrent symptoms after prolonged exposure to cotton dust are more likely to show an obstructive pattern on pulmonary function tests.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ORGANIC DUSTS

items.question: Which condition results from exposure to moldy hay containing spores of thermophilic actinomycetes?
items.explanation: Exposure to moldy hay containing spores of thermophilic actinomycetes leads to farmer's lung, a type of hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ORGANIC DUSTS

items.question: Which toxic agent is associated with polymer fume fever when inhaled after high-temperature heating?
items.explanation: Inhalation of volatilized Teflon at high temperatures causes polymer fume fever.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · TOXIC CHEMICALS

items.question: What is a common cause of acute cardiorespiratory failure in firefighters?
items.explanation: Smoke inhalation, not thermal injury, is a more common cause of acute cardiorespiratory failure in firefighters.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · TOXIC CHEMICALS

items.question: Which condition has been linked to occupational exposure to diacetyl?
items.explanation: Diacetyl exposure has been associated with bronchiolitis obliterans.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · TOXIC CHEMICALS

items.question: What type of lung disease is a concern for veterans exposed to burn pit emissions?
items.explanation: Interests in interstitial lung diseases, including granulomatous types, are concerns for veterans exposed to burn pit emissions.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · TOXIC CHEMICALS

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY
items.question: Which occupational exposure is associated with sarcomas?
items.explanation: Vinyl chloride has been linked to the development of sarcomas.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY
items.question: What is required by the U.S. Social Security Administration for income replacement payments due to disability?
items.explanation: The U.S. Social Security Administration requires total disability before providing income replacement payments.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY
items.question: Which pulmonary function test is used as the initial assessment tool for respiratory impairment rating?
items.explanation: Resting pulmonary function tests, including spirometry and diffusing capacity, are used initially for respiratory impairment rating.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY

items.question: Which pollutants are regulated by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) under primary standards?
options: Sulfur dioxide, particulate matter, nitrogen dioxide, ozone, lead, and carbon monoxide
Carbon dioxide, methane, chlorofluorocarbons, sulfur hexafluoride, and nitrous oxide
Ammonia, hydrogen sulfide, radon, formaldehyde, and benzene
Volatile organic compounds, asbestos, mercury, dioxins, and polychlorinated biphenyls
items.explanation: The EPA regulates sulfur dioxide, particulate matter, nitrogen dioxide, ozone, lead, and carbon monoxide under primary standards.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OUTDOOR AIR POLLUTION

items.question: Which of the following is a documented effect of chronic exposure to traffic-related air pollution?
options: Decreased growth of lung function and increased asthma
items.explanation: Chronic exposure to traffic-related air pollution is associated with decreased growth of lung function and increased asthma.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OUTDOOR AIR POLLUTION

items.question: What mechanism has been proposed for the cardiovascular effects of fine particulate matter (PM) inhalation?
options: Direct damage to heart muscle cells
Oxidative stress leading to local injury and systemic inflammation
items.explanation: Inhalation of fine PM generates oxidative stress, causing local lung injury and systemic inflammatory responses.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OUTDOOR AIR POLLUTION

items.question: Which indoor exposure is associated with a ~25% increase in relative risk for lung cancer and cardiopulmonary diseases, even after adjustment for major potential confounders?
items.explanation: Recent meta-analyses suggest a ~25% increase in relative risk for lung cancer and cardiopulmonary diseases due to secondhand tobacco smoke.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · INDOOR EXPOSURES

items.question: What is the main radon product that poses a risk factor for lung cancer?
items.explanation: The main radon product is radon-222, which results from the decay of uranium-238.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · INDOOR EXPOSURES

items.question: Which indoor exposure has been associated with an increased risk of asthma due to oxides of nitrogen?
items.explanation: Exposure to oxides of nitrogen from gas appliances has been associated with an increased risk of asthma.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · INDOOR EXPOSURES

items.question: What percentage of the world's population uses solid fuel for cooking, heating, or baking?
items.explanation: Forty percent of the world's population uses solid fuel for these purposes.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · INDOOR EXPOSURES

items.subtopic: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT
items.statement: OSHA regulations require employers to inform workers about potential hazards in their workplaces.
items.explanation: Under OSHA regulations, employers must provide information about potential hazardous agents through Safety Data Sheets and training.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT

items.subtopic: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT
items.statement: Current environmental measurements alone are sufficient to assess a patient's past exposure.
items.explanation: Chronic diseases from long-term exposures require combining current measurements with work histories for accurate assessment of past exposure.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease | HISTORY AND EXPOSURE ASSESSMENT

items.statement: The ILO system is always reliable for assessing the severity of lung impairment in individual workers.
items.explanation: The ILO system can be problematic when applied to an individual worker's chest radiograph.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · LABORATORY TESTS

items.statement: High-resolution CT improves the detection of interstitial changes in lung disease.
items.explanation: HRCT is noted for improving the detection of interstitial changes compared to conventional CT.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · LABORATORY TESTS

items.statement: Particles >10–15 μm in diameter are likely to deposit beyond the nose and throat.
items.explanation: Particles >10–15 μm do not penetrate beyond the nose and throat.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · DETERMINANTS OF INHALATIONAL EXPOSURE

items.statement: The fine-mode fraction of particles is primarily created by high-temperature industrial processes.
items.explanation: Fine particles (<2.5 μm) are created by burning fossil fuels or high-temperature industrial processes.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · DETERMINANTS OF INHALATIONAL EXPOSURE

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES
items.statement: Asbestosis can manifest after less than 10 years of asbestos exposure.
items.explanation: Asbestosis usually manifests after at least 10 years of exposure to asbestos.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES
items.statement: Mesotheliomas are associated with a significant interactive effect between smoking and asbestos exposure.
items.explanation: Unlike lung cancer, mesotheliomas are not associated with smoking.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES

items.statement: Silica has immunoadjuvant properties that can lead to autoimmune connective tissue disorders.
items.explanation: The passage states silica's immunoadjuvant properties may result in autoimmune conditions like rheumatoid arthritis and scleroderma.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · SILICOSIS

items.statement: Patients with silicosis are at a decreased risk of lung infections.
items.explanation: Silica causes alveolar macrophage dysfunction, increasing the risk of lung infections.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · SILICOSIS

items.statement: Simple radiographically identified CWP is seen in approximately 50% of all coal miners.
items.explanation: Simple CWP is seen in ~10% of all coal miners, not 50%.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · COAL WORKER'S PNEUMOCONIOSIS (CWP)

items.statement: The effects of coal dust exposure are additive to those of cigarette smoking.
items.explanation: Coal dust exposure and cigarette smoking have additive effects on lung health.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · COAL WORKER'S PNEUMOCONIOSIS (CWP)

items.statement: Chronic beryllium disease can be distinguished from sarcoidosis by the presence of hilar adenopathy.
items.explanation: Hilar adenopathy is somewhat less common in chronic beryllium disease compared to sarcoidosis.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · CHRONIC BERYLLIUM DISEASE

items.statement: Fiberoptic bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy is usually required to diagnose chronic beryllium disease.
items.explanation: Diagnosis of chronic beryllium disease often requires fiberoptic bronchoscopy with transbronchial lung biopsy to identify noncaseating granulomas or monocytic infiltration.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · CHRONIC BERYLLIUM DISEASE

items.statement: Cigarette smoking is a minor cause of chronic mucus hypersecretion.
items.explanation: Cigarette smoking is the major cause of conditions associated with chronic mucus hypersecretion.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ☐ OTHER METALS | OTHER INORGANIC DUSTS

items.statement: Small airway inflammation may be the initial site of pathologic response in cases involving irritant dust and smoking.
items.explanation: The pattern of the irritant dust effect is similar to that of cigarette smoking, suggesting small airway inflammation as an initial site.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ☐ OTHER METALS | OTHER INORGANIC DUSTS

items.statement: Grain dust exposure alone is the primary cause of obstructive airway disease in exposed workers.
items.explanation: The effect of grain dust exposure on obstructive airway disease is additive to that of cigarette smoking.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ORGANIC DUSTS

items.statement: Regular surveillance of pulmonary function using spirometry before and after the work shift is required by OSHA for cotton dust-exposed workers.
items.explanation: OSHA mandates regular surveillance of pulmonary function in cotton dust-exposed workers.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ORGANIC DUSTS

items.statement: Carbon monoxide poisoning can lead to significant hypoxemia and be life-threatening.
items.explanation: Carbon monoxide poisoning results in significant hypoxemia, which is potentially life-threatening.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · TOXIC CHEMICALS

items.statement: IgE antibody-mediated mechanisms are necessary for occupational asthma caused by diisocyanates.
items.explanation: Occupational asthma from diisocyanates does not necessarily involve an IgE antibody-mediated mechanism.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · TOXIC CHEMICALS

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY
items.statement: Workers' compensation systems in many states allow payments only for total disability.
items.explanation: Many state workers' compensation systems allow for payments for partial disability.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY
items.statement: The assessment of disability involves only medical factors.
items.explanation: Assessment of disability also involves nonmedical factors such as education and employability.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY

items.statement: Photochemically derived pollution is only a problem in Los Angeles.
items.explanation: Photochemically derived pollution, or smog, is now known to be a problem throughout the United States and many other countries.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OUTDOOR AIR POLLUTION

items.statement: Recent research findings have led to stricter U.S. ambient air quality standards for ozone, oxides of nitrogen, and particulate matter.
items.explanation: Stricter standards were implemented due to recent research on the health effects of these pollutants.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OUTDOOR AIR POLLUTION

items.statement: Household air pollution from cooking or heating with solid fuels is responsible for a significant percentage of deaths worldwide.
items.explanation: Indoor exposure to household air pollution is estimated to be responsible for 4% of deaths globally.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · INDOOR EXPOSURES

items.statement: The main radon product, radon-222, has no significant impact on lung cancer risk.
items.explanation: Radon gas in homes is a known risk factor for lung cancer.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · INDOOR EXPOSURES

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES
items.assertion: Asbestos exposure can lead to pleural plaques.
items.reason: Pleural plaques are characterized by thickening or calcification along the parietal pleura and imply only exposure, not pulmonary impairment.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why pleural plaques indicate asbestos exposure.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES
items.assertion: Asbestos is still widely used in developed countries.
items.reason: Synthetic mineral fibers have largely replaced asbestos in the developed world.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because synthetic mineral fibers have largely replaced asbestos in the developed world.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL EXPOSURES AND PULMONARY DISEASE | ASBESTOS-RELATED DISEASES

items.assertion: Cobalt exposure can lead to occupational asthma.
items.reason: Cobalt is a constituent of tungsten carbide and is the likely etiologic agent of interstitial pneumonitis and occupational asthma.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ☐ OTHER METALS | OTHER INORGANIC DUSTS

items.assertion: Farmer's lung can be differentiated from influenza or pneumonia based on the history of exposure.
items.reason: A patient with acute farmer's lung presents with fever, chills, malaise, cough, and dyspnea without wheezing after exposure to moldy hay.
items.explanation: The temporal relation of symptoms to exposure is crucial in diagnosing farmer's lung, distinguishing it from other conditions like influenza or pneumonia.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ORGANIC DUSTS

items.assertion: Endotoxin levels are associated with the risk of byssinosis.
items.reason: Byssinosis is characterized by chest tightness and a drop in FEV1 over the course of a Monday work shift.
items.explanation: While endotoxin levels contribute to the risk, the characterization of symptoms like chest tightness explains the clinical presentation but not the association with endotoxin.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · ORGANIC DUSTS

items.assertion: Firefighters are at risk of smoke inhalation, which can cause acute cardiorespiratory failure.
items.reason: Smoke inhalation kills more fire victims than thermal injury does.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why firefighters are at risk.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · TOXIC CHEMICALS

items.assertion: World Trade Center dust exposure led to new cough and wheeze syndromes among local residents.
items.reason: The high alkalinity of WTC dust caused significant respiratory symptoms in firefighters and cleanup crews.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why new cough and wheeze syndromes occurred among local residents.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · TOXIC CHEMICALS

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY
items.assertion: The U.S. Social Security Administration requires a determination of cause for disability payments.
items.reason: No determination of cause is done in the Social Security scheme; work-relatedness must be established in workers' compensation systems.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because no determination of cause is required by the U.S. Social Security Administration.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · OCCUPATIONAL RESPIRATORY CARCINOGENS | ASSESSMENT OF DISABILITY

items.assertion: Exposure to biomass smoke can lead to chronic respiratory effects.
items.reason: Increased migration from developing countries brings awareness of the chronic respiratory effects of exposure to biomass smoke, including interstitial lung disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · INDOOR EXPOSURES

items.assertion: Household air pollution contributes to outdoor air pollution in mega-cities.
items.reason: Contributions from household air pollution, coal-fired power plants without emission scrubbers, and increased traffic congestion lead to high concentrations of fine PM.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Occupational and Environmental Lung Disease · INDOOR EXPOSURES
